questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what is the difference between Python's `is` and `==`?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar.

answers.text: From what I've seen, `is` checks object identity.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `is` checks object identity.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `is` checks object identity.

questions.prompt: No searching: what happens after `b = a` when `a` is a list?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Both names refer to the same list object.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Both names refer to the same list object.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Both names refer to the same list object.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Python automatically copies the list.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Assignment binds another name to the same object; copying must be requested.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Assignment binds another name to the same object; copying must be requested.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: does Python 3 `range(1_000_000)` create one million integer objects immediately?

answers.text: Short version: Yes, it stores every integer in a list.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A range stores compact start, stop, and step information.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A range stores compact start, stop, and step information.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Range values are produced as needed during iteration.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Range values are produced as needed during iteration.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Range values are produced as needed during iteration.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: a range accepts floating-point start, stop, and step arguments.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The built-in range requires integer arguments or objects with an integer index interpretation.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The built-in range requires integer arguments or objects with an integer index interpretation.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: why can a mutable default argument surprise Python developers?

answers.text: My answer: Repeated calls can share the same mutable default object.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Repeated calls can share the same mutable default object.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Repeated calls can share the same mutable default object.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Using `None` and creating the list inside the function avoids shared state.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Using `None` and creating the list inside the function avoids shared state.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Using `None` and creating the list inside the function avoids shared state.

answers.text: The way I understand it: the default object is created once when the function is defined.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The default object is created once when the function is defined.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The default object is created once when the function is defined.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: in a GIL-enabled CPython build, what does the Global Interpreter Lock imply for threads?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Threads can never improve an I/O-bound program.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Other threads can run while one waits for I/O, and extensions may release the GIL.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Other threads can run while one waits for I/O, and extensions may release the GIL.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: how does a Python generator differ from a returned list?

answers.text: The practical answer is: it produces values lazily as it is iterated.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It produces values lazily as it is iterated.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It produces values lazily as it is iterated.

answers.text: My answer: It can always be restarted after exhaustion.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A generator iterator is normally exhausted after one pass.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A generator iterator is normally exhausted after one pass.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: A generator supports `len()` because it knows all future values.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A generator produces values lazily and does not generally provide a length.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A generator produces values lazily and does not generally provide a length.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what ordering guarantee do modern Python dictionaries provide?

answers.text: The practical answer is: they automatically sort keys.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Insertion order is preserved, but keys are not automatically sorted.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Insertion order is preserved, but keys are not automatically sorted.

answers.text: My answer: Updating an existing key does not move it to the end.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Updating an existing key does not move it to the end.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Updating an existing key does not move it to the end.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: They preserve insertion order.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They preserve insertion order.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They preserve insertion order.

questions.prompt: Forum check: what happens if blocking code runs directly inside an asyncio coroutine?

answers.text: From what I've seen, `await` only yields when the awaited operation cooperates with the event loop.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `await` only yields when the awaited operation cooperates with the event loop.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `await` only yields when the awaited operation cooperates with the event loop.

answers.text: The practical answer is: it can block the event loop and delay other tasks.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It can block the event loop and delay other tasks.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It can block the event loop and delay other tasks.

answers.text: My answer: The event loop automatically moves every blocking call to a thread.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Blocking work must be explicitly offloaded or replaced with async I/O.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Blocking work must be explicitly offloaded or replaced with async I/O.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what is a Python context manager responsible for?

answers.text: The practical answer is: it defines setup and cleanup around a `with` block.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It defines setup and cleanup around a `with` block.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It defines setup and cleanup around a `with` block.

answers.text: My answer: It only works with files.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Locks, transactions, temporary resources, and custom objects can use the protocol.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Locks, transactions, temporary resources, and custom objects can use the protocol.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: A context manager automatically suppresses every exception raised inside the block.

feedback.legit: Not this one. An exception is suppressed only when the exit method explicitly returns a true value.
feedback.sus: Good catch. An exception is suppressed only when the exit method explicitly returns a true value.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: what can a Python descriptor customize?

answers.text: The practical answer is: it only changes how dictionary keys are looked up.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Descriptors participate in object attribute access, not normal mapping lookup.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Descriptors participate in object attribute access, not normal mapping lookup.

answers.text: My answer: Properties are implemented using the descriptor protocol.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Properties are implemented using the descriptor protocol.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Properties are implemented using the descriptor protocol.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: It can customize attribute access through methods such as `__get__` and `__set__`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It can customize attribute access through methods such as `__get__` and `__set__`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It can customize attribute access through methods such as `__get__` and `__set__`.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: how is Python's method resolution order used with multiple inheritance?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, zero-argument `super()` follows the MRO rather than naming one fixed parent.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Zero-argument `super()` follows the MRO rather than naming one fixed parent.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Zero-argument `super()` follows the MRO rather than naming one fixed parent.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: It gives a consistent linear order for attribute lookup.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It gives a consistent linear order for attribute lookup.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It gives a consistent linear order for attribute lookup.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Python searches base classes in a random order.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Python computes a deterministic C3 linearization.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Python computes a deterministic C3 linearization.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: does `@dataclass(frozen=True)` make an entire object graph immutable?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Yes, every nested object becomes immutable.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Frozen prevents normal field reassignment but does not freeze nested mutable objects.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Frozen prevents normal field reassignment but does not freeze nested mutable objects.

answers.text: The way I understand it: a frozen dataclass automatically deep-copies mutable constructor arguments.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Frozen dataclasses do not deep-copy their fields; mutable objects passed to the constructor remain mutable.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Frozen dataclasses do not deep-copy their fields; mutable objects passed to the constructor remain mutable.

answers.text: Short version: Every frozen dataclass instance is safely hashable regardless of its fields.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Generated hashing still depends on the dataclass options and on whether component values are hashable.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Generated hashing still depends on the dataclass options and on whether component values are hashable.
